START.text: Good evening. Let's take an honest look at your day — not to judge, but to understand.

Q1.text: When something didn't go as planned today, what was your first reaction?
options: I thought about what I could have done differently
I felt frustrated by things outside my control
I adapted and found another way
I waited for someone else to fix it

Q2_INTERNAL.text: When you reflect on a setback today, where did your mind go first?
options: What I could control and change
What I learned from it
How to prevent it next time

Q2_EXTERNAL.text: When things went wrong today, what felt most true?
options: The situation was genuinely unfair
I didn't have the support I needed
I'm not sure I had any real choice

REF_LOCUS_AGENCY.text: Focusing on what you can control is the foundation of resilience. That instinct is worth protecting.

REF_LOCUS_GROWTH.text: Treating setbacks as data is a growth mindset in practice. You're building something durable.

REF_LOCUS_PROACTIVE.text: Thinking ahead after a stumble is how patterns get broken. That's agency at work.

REF_LOCUS_UNFAIR.text: Some situations are genuinely unfair — that's real. The question worth sitting with: was there any small choice available to you, even inside the unfairness?

REF_LOCUS_SUPPORT.text: Needing support is legitimate. And noticing the gap is the first step to either asking for it or building it yourself.

REF_LOCUS_CHOICE.text: There's almost always a micro-choice available — even if it's just how you interpret what happened. That's not blame. That's power.

BRIDGE_1_2.text: Now let's shift from how you responded to what you gave today.

Q3.text: Which of these best describes a moment from your day?
options: I helped someone with something that wasn't my responsibility
I felt I deserved more recognition for my work
I did something extra without being asked
I felt frustrated that others weren't pulling their weight

Q4_CONTRIBUTION.text: When you gave that extra effort today, what was driving it?
options: I genuinely wanted to help
I saw a gap and filled it
It felt like the right thing to do

Q4_ENTITLEMENT.text: When you felt that frustration or unmet expectation, what did you do with it?
options: I let it sit and it affected my mood
I brought it up with someone
I tried to refocus on what I could do

REF_CONTRIB_GENUINE.text: Helping because you want to — not because you have to — is what builds real trust on a team.

REF_CONTRIB_GAP.text: Seeing what needs doing and doing it without being asked is rare. That's organizational citizenship in action.

REF_CONTRIB_VALUES.text: Acting from values rather than obligation is a sign of character. That kind of consistency compounds.

REF_ENTITLE_PASSIVE.text: Unspoken frustration is expensive — it costs you energy and changes nothing. Tomorrow, try naming it, even just to yourself.

REF_ENTITLE_VOCAL.text: Raising it is better than holding it. The question is whether the conversation was about what you need — or what you could give.

REF_ENTITLE_REDIRECT.text: Catching yourself and refocusing is real self-awareness. That pivot from 'what I deserve' to 'what I can do' is the whole game.

BRIDGE_2_3.text: One more lens. Let's look at how wide your circle of concern was today.

Q5.text: When you think about today's biggest challenge, who comes to mind?
options: Just me — it was my problem to solve
My team — we were all affected
A specific colleague who had it harder than me
The customer or end user we're serving

Q6_SELF.text: While you were working through that challenge, did anyone else cross your mind?
options: Not really — I was heads down
Briefly, but I stayed focused on my part
Yes, I wondered how it was affecting others

Q6_TEAM.text: When you thought about the team being affected, what did you do?
options: I checked in with someone
I adjusted my work to reduce their load
I kept it in mind but focused on my own tasks

Q6_OTHER.text: When you noticed that colleague had it harder, what happened next?
options: I offered help or support
I felt grateful for my own situation
I wanted to help but wasn't sure how

Q6_BEYOND.text: Keeping the end user in mind — how did that shape your work today?
options: It gave me a reason to push through difficulty
It helped me prioritize what actually mattered
It reminded me why the work is worth doing

REF_RADIUS_NARROW.text: Deep focus has its place. But tomorrow, try one moment of looking up — noticing how your work lands on someone else.

REF_RADIUS_EMERGING.text: That brief awareness of others is worth expanding. The more you widen your lens, the more your work feels meaningful.

REF_RADIUS_EXPANDING.text: Wondering how challenges affect others is the beginning of perspective-taking. That instinct makes you a better teammate.

REF_RADIUS_CHECKIN.text: Checking in is a small act with outsized impact. People remember who showed up when things were hard.

REF_RADIUS_ADJUSTED.text: Adjusting your work to ease someone else's load — that's what high-trust teams are built on.

REF_RADIUS_AWARE.text: Awareness without action is still a start. Tomorrow, let that awareness move you one step further.

REF_RADIUS_HELPED.text: Offering help when you notice someone struggling — that's Maslow's self-transcendence in everyday form.

REF_RADIUS_GRATEFUL.text: Gratitude that comes from seeing others' struggles is grounding. Let it also move you toward them.

REF_RADIUS_HESITANT.text: Wanting to help but not knowing how is still care. Next time, a simple 'how are you holding up?' is enough.

REF_RADIUS_PURPOSE.text: Using the end user as fuel to push through — that's purpose-driven work. It's more sustainable than willpower alone.

REF_RADIUS_CLARITY.text: Letting the end user guide your priorities is a form of wisdom. It cuts through noise fast.

REF_RADIUS_MEANING.text: Meaning is found in contribution to something beyond yourself. You found it today.

SUMMARY.text: Today you reflected across three dimensions: where you locate control, whether you led with contribution or expectation, and how wide your circle of concern reached. Every honest answer moves the needle.

END.text: Rest well. Tomorrow you get to choose again.
